prompt:
bodyparser.ts is the intake gateway's body-parsing module — JSON only today, works fine, several services call it. Now the kiosk contact forms (classic urlencoded posts, one of the older kiosk builds still submits latin-1) and the expense-report uploads (multipart with a receipt file) are moving onto the gateway, so it needs to grow two parsers and per-type size caps. Same entry point, parseBody(raw, contentType, opts) — handlers keep switching on the result's type. The EXISTING BEHAVIOR block at the top of test_bodyparser.ts already passes and MUST stay green; every current call site passes { maxBytes } positionally like today.

1. application/x-www-form-urlencoded → { type: 'form', fields } with fields as Record<string, string[]>: repeated keys accumulate in order, keys appear in first-appearance order, a bare key (no '=') gets the empty string, empty segments between '&'s are skipped, '+' means space in both keys and values BEFORE percent-decoding. Charset parameter: utf-8 is the default and always fine; iso-8859-1 (alias latin1) must work for the old kiosks — percent escapes decode to bytes, then the bytes decode per that charset; anything else is a BodyParseError 'bad-charset'. A malformed escape (%G1, truncated %2) or percent-decoded bytes that aren't valid in the charset are 'bad-encoding'.

2. multipart/form-data → { type: 'multipart', fields, files }. The boundary parameter is required (quoted or not) — missing means 'bad-multipart'. Parts without a filename are text fields (utf-8, invalid bytes are 'bad-encoding'); parts with a filename go to files as { field, filename, contentType (default application/octet-stream), data } with data as raw untouched bytes — never charset-decode a file. Part header names are case-insensitive; Content-Disposition params may be quoted or bare tokens; filenames arrive as raw utf-8 and accented names like reçu-café.pdf must come through intact. Ignore any preamble before the first delimiter and anything after the closing one. Strictness where it matters: no closing --boundary-- delimiter, or a part missing a usable Content-Disposition name, is 'bad-multipart'. Keep it CRLF-only — this is browser output, don't get clever with lone newlines.

3. Size caps per type: opts gains limits?: { json?, urlencoded?, multipart? } in bytes. The cap for a body is its type's entry when present, else maxBytes, else the existing 1 MiB default — checked against the RAW byte length before any parsing, throwing 'too-large'.

Everything still throws BodyParseError with a stable code, and text/plain etc. remain 'unsupported-type'. The full contract is the one test file: node --test test_bodyparser.ts all green, test file read-only, node stdlib only.
